# 01. Geese / Chaotic Good trend simulation

> **Use boundary:** This file is written for research, ethics, detection, and policy analysis. It describes mechanisms at a conceptual level so they can be studied, criticised, or detected. It is not an execution guide for manipulating platforms, audiences, investors, advertisers, or creators.


## Domain

Music / TikTok / culture

## Evidence type

Reported marketing campaign and public debate

## What the case demonstrates

Trend-perception engineering: distributed accounts/pages, clips, comments and UGC-like interactions make a song/artist appear culturally in motion.

## What is known

WIRED reported Chaotic Good helped distribute clips for Geese/Cameron Winter and described “trend simulation”; TechCrunch quoted the idea of posting enough volume across enough accounts to simulate a song trending.

## What is not known / caution

Do not overstate beyond the source record. Distinguish documented enforcement, platform policy, reputable reporting, and unresolved allegations.

## Signal layers involved

- Visibility: whether the object appears widely seen or discussed.
- Credibility: whether observers infer independent validation.
- Conversion: whether apparent interest turns into sales, streams, installs, or signups.
- Attribution: whether the correct actor gets credit.
- Legitimacy: whether the signal becomes status or institutional trust.

## Ethical problem

The ethical issue is not simply that marketing exists. It is that the audience, platform, or market may infer genuine demand from signals whose origin, independence, or quality has been distorted.

## Evidence strength

Strong for current cultural example; not a regulator finding. Use as reputable reporting and industry-language evidence.

## Slide use

Use this case to show a concrete version of the thesis: a proxy signal was used as evidence, but the path by which that signal was produced complicates or corrupts the inference.

## Source notes

- **WIRED — The Fanfare Around the Band Geese Actually Was a Psyop** (News / cultural reporting). Reports on Chaotic Good Projects, Geese, networks of social pages, TikTok recommendation-seeding, burner accounts/comments, and the phrase “trend simulation”. URL: https://www.wired.com/story/geese-chaotic-good-marketing-industry-plant/
- **TechCrunch — Everything we like is a psyop** (News / analysis). Includes quoted explanation of posting enough volume across enough accounts to simulate a song trending; describes Phia creator-farm strategy and volume of creator videos. URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/16/everything-we-like-is-a-psyop/
